coredata.dc:title: Designing of chitosan/gelatin based nanocomposite films integrated with Vachellia nilotica gum carbon dots for smart food packaging applications
coredata.dc:creator: Parveen, Shehla
Nazeer, Sadia
Chotana, Ghayoor Abbas
Kanwal, Aqsa
Batool, Benish
Bukhari, Naeema
Yaqoob, Asma
Talib, Faiza
coredata.dc:description: Microbial growth and exposure to UV light is a persistent global concern resulting in food spoilage, therefore, smart packaging is crucial for the availability of safer and quality food. Present work describes fabrication of chitosan (CH) and gelatin (GL) based nanocomposite films by introducing green source, highly fluorescent Vachillia nilotica gum-derived carbon dots (VNG-CDs). The VNG-CDs and incorporated CH/GL nanocomposite films were characterized by UV–Visible, FTIR, XRD, SEM and TGA analysis. The FTIR and XRD data revealed that VNG-CDs, chitosan, gelatin, and glycerol are combined/interlinked to form homogeneous nanocomposite films. The inclusion of VNG-CDs to CS/GL-CDs nanocomposite film efficiently enhanced the thermal stability and improved mechanical properties. VNG-CDs added to films markedly blocked the ultraviolet light and their effectiveness improved as concentration of CDs increases, being >90 % in UVC (200–280 nm) region. The prepared CS/GL-CDs nanocomposite films manifested radical scavenging activity, reducing capability and also excellently inhibited growth of E. coli, K. pneumonia and S. aureus bacteria. The viability of CS/GL-CDs nanocomposite films examined using banana as a model fruit extending the storage time by two weeks. In conclusion, CH/GL films containing VNG-CDs can be developed into smart packaging materials with enhanced protection and antimicrobial properties.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Cationic carbon dots
Nanocomposite films
Environmentally sustainable
Antibacterial
UV-blocking properties
Smart food packaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Thickness, water solubility and water vapor permeability of CS/GL-CDs nanocomposite films
Packaging application of CS/GL nanocomposite films on real sample (banana)
Water solubility and water vapor permeability
Optical properties (UV visible and transmittance) of CH/GL-CDs nanocomposite films
Antibacterial activities of VNG-CDs and nanocomposite films
Antioxidant and reducing activities of VNG-CDs and nanocomposite films
Application of nanocomposite films to monitor banana freshness

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Design strategy for fluorescent VNG-CDs integrated films for active packaging applications.

Optical properties of VNG-CDs a) UV–Visible spectrum of carbon dots inset: GUM spectrum b) PL Emission spectrum of CDs with green fluorescence435–413. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Optical properties of VNG-CDs a) PL emission spectra at various wavelengths b) PL emission spectra at different dilutions.
SEM images and size distribution histogram of VNG-CDs.
a) FTIR spectrum; b) XRD graph c) Thermo gravimetric analysis and d) cytotoxicity of VNG-CDs.
a) UV–Visible spectra and b) % Transmittance spectra of VNG-CDs.
FTIR spectra of a) CH/GL control film b) CH/GL-CDs1% c) CH/GL-CDs3% d) CH/GL-CDs5%.
XRD graphs of CH/GL film and CH/GL-CDs1–5 % nanocomposite films.
Thermo gravimetric analysis of VNG-CDs, CH/GL film and CH/GL-CDs5%.
Antibacterial Activities of VNG-CDs and CH/GL-CDs1–5 %.
a) DPPH radical scavenging assay b) Reducing capability (Total Phenolic Content as mgGAE/g) of VNG-CDs, CH/GL film and CH/GL-CDs1–5 %.
Application of VNG-CDs-incorporated CH/GL films for banana preservation.
Thickness, water solubility and water vapor permeability of CH/GL and nanocomposite films.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: a-eThe results are reported as mean ± S.D. Data was analyzed by one-way variance analysis (ANOVA) and Tukey multiple comparison test (p < 0.05).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Transmittance values of CS/GL and CS/GL-CDs nanocomposite films at different wavelengths (255, 300, 340 and 540 nm).

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: a-fThe results are reported as mean ± S.D. Data was analyzed by one-way variance analysis (ANOVA) and Tukey multiple comparison test (p < 0.05).

head.ce:title: Designing of chitosan/gelatin based nanocomposite films integrated with Vachellia nilotica gum carbon dots for smart food packaging applications

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, The Government Sadiq College Women University Bahawalpur, Bahawalpur 63100, Pakistan

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: The Government Sadiq College Women University Bahawalpur

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, The Government Sadiq College Women University Bahawalpur, Bahawalpur 63100, Pakistan

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Sayyed Babar Ali School of science and Engineering, Lahore University of Management Sciences, Lahore 54792, Pakistan

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Sayyed Babar Ali School of science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Sayyed Babar Ali School of science and Engineering, Lahore University of Management Sciences, Lahore 54792, Pakistan

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Biohemistry, Institute of biochemistry biotechnology and bio-informatics, Baghdad-ul-Jadeed Campus, The Islamia University of Bahawalpur, Bahawalpur 63100, Pakistan

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of biochemistry biotechnology and bio-informatics

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Biohemistry, Institute of biochemistry biotechnology and bio-informatics, Baghdad-ul-Jadeed Campus, The Islamia University of Bahawalpur, Bahawalpur 63100, Pakistan

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Microbial growth and exposure to UV light is a persistent global concern resulting in food spoilage, therefore, smart packaging is crucial for the availability of safer and quality food. Present work describes fabrication of chitosan (CH) and gelatin (GL) based nanocomposite films by introducing green source, highly fluorescent Vachillia nilotica gum-derived carbon dots (VNG-CDs). The VNG-CDs and incorporated CH/GL nanocomposite films were characterized by UV–Visible, FTIR, XRD, SEM and TGA analysis. The FTIR and XRD data revealed that VNG-CDs, chitosan, gelatin, and glycerol are combined/interlinked to form homogeneous nanocomposite films. The inclusion of VNG-CDs to CS/GL-CDs nanocomposite film efficiently enhanced the thermal stability and improved mechanical properties. VNG-CDs added to films markedly blocked the ultraviolet light and their effectiveness improved as concentration of CDs increases, being >90 % in UVC (200–280 nm) region. The prepared CS/GL-CDs nanocomposite films manifested radical scavenging activity, reducing capability and also excellently inhibited growth of E. coli, K. pneumonia and S. aureus bacteria. The viability of CS/GL-CDs nanocomposite films examined using banana as a model fruit extending the storage time by two weeks. In conclusion, CH/GL films containing VNG-CDs can be developed into smart packaging materials with enhanced protection and antimicrobial properties.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Packaging serves as an essential protector, actively safeguarding its contents from external factors that can negatively impact its quality and safety. This includes physical safety and protection from environmental elements like moisture, air, light, and contaminants. It should also create a barrier from microbial adulteration, which can lead to food borne illnesses and spoilage, as well as should prevent spoilage, reduce the risk of contamination, and extend the shelf life of perishable foods [1]. Plastics are widely utilized in packaging industries for their cost-effectiveness, portability, versatility, lightweight nature, strength, protective qualities, and quick manufacturing [2]. Global manufacturing of plastics reached 400 million metric tons (Mt) in 2018 and 8 billion Mt. over all [3]. However, plastic materials are not degradable and cause serious problems to human health and the environment. According to recent data, the world generates 381 million tons of polymeric trash each year, of which 50 % comes from plastics [4].

Currently, there is an increasing recognition of alternative biopolymers to replace plastic as packaging materials. The scientific community has given much attention to biopolymers due to the development of biodegradable films and coatings [5].Two biopolymers, chitosan and gelatin offer a potent combination of biodegradability, film-forming properties, non-toxicity, and compatibility with various bioactive compounds, allowing them to serve as essential materials in food, pharmaceutical, and other product applications [6]. Chitosan is a charged biopolymer that contains a mixture of N-acetyl glucosamine and β-(1 → 4 linked) d-glucosamine [5]. The cationic groups in its structure display antimicrobial activity against bacteria, yeasts, molds, and fungi [7,8]. Gelatin is a natural protein that has no distinct taste or color, easily dissolves in water and is obtained through either complete or partial disintegration of collagen originating from various sources (hides, skins, and bones) at a low cost and with high availability [9]. Gelatin has remarkable gel-forming characteristics, and physical attributes such as the ability to absorb ultraviolet light due to the presence of aromatic amino acids like tryptophan and tyrosine, control moisture permeation, and heat transfer [10]. Gelatin possesses good gaseous insulation and swelling capabilities but weak cohesion and sensitivity to moisture. The inadequate moisture resistance property of gelatin prevents its application in packaging supplies. This limitation can be effectively overcome by blending it with a compatible natural polymer [11]. Earlier research suggested that incorporating chitosan into gelatin creates an effective gas barrier against CO2 and O2 due to its strong mechanical strength, bioactivity, and lower moisture transfer related to pure gelatin. Both Chitosan and Gelatin are hydrophilic biopolymers known for their natural compatibility and strong affinity, blending them into a composite film has the potential to improve both mechanical strength and water resistance when, compared to individual component films [12,13].

It is crucial to develop innovative packaging films to enhance food preservation, safety, and sustainability [14]. The creation of biopolymer nanocomposite films signifies a promising approach that leverages the benefits of natural polymers and nanoscale fillers (including nanoparticles and carbon dots). This method not only enhances the functionality of packaging films, providing attributes such as oxygen barrier strength, water vapor permeability, antimicrobial and antioxidant properties but also ensures that these materials remain recyclable and environment friendly [15]. Roy, et al., synthesized carrageenan-based films by incorporating silver nanoparticles to improve disinfection. The reported films have good stability of temperature, strength, rigidity and antibacterial activity [16]. Roy et al. [17] used ZnO nanoparticles and essential oil emulsion for fabrication of gelatin/agar based nanocomposite film which shown excellent antibacterial action in the presence of ZnO NPs and PEC, with a 100 % suppression of L. monocytogenes and a 50 % drop in the E. coli population [17].

Additionally, food materials are also frequently subjected to solar UV radiation, which can induce oxidative damage and potentially have a detrimental impact on the shelf life of beverages by causing alterations in their taste, odor, or color [18]. Milk-related items comprising vitamin A & B12 eliminate several important nutrients once encountered by ultraviolet rays because the potent photo sensitizer generate extremely reactive oxygen radicals [19,20]. Carbon dots are gaining significant attention as nano fillers in the improvement of CH/GL film properties, owing to their cost-effectiveness, UV-blocking abilities, superior stability, minimal environmental impact, antioxidant properties, and antimicrobial functions [21,22]. Sul et al., fabricated chitosan and gelatin based films by incorporating banana peel-derived carbon dots, demonstrating high antioxidant (74 % DPPH & 99 % ABTS), UV blocking characteristics and antibacterial action against pathogenic bacteria L. monocytogenes, completely inhibiting their growth within 6 h and indicating their utility in meat preservation and shelf-life extension [23]. Khan and his co-workers utilized CDs manufactured from green tea in combination with gelatin and chitosan to create active packaging film for prolonging the pig meat storage. They report that CDs-based CH/GL films demonstrated remarkable ultraviolet-defense abilities (UVA: 90.2 % & UVB: 99.6 %), while films containing 3 % CDs demonstrated great antibacterial and anti-oxidant efficiency, achieving 99.2 % and 63.8 % with ABTS and DPPH, respectively [24]. Zhao, et al., utilized banana based CDs and aqueous solution of chitosan (0.16 %) for broadening preservation period along with stability for soy milk. These CDs had anti-microbial effectiveness towards B. subtilis, S. aureus & E. coli [25]. Cuevas and his colleagues developed a cellulose-based films modified with both CDs and nitrogen-doped CDs. They concluded that the presence of a wider range of chemical clusters (including amino and amides) on the surface of the carbon dots enhanced film conductivity, resulting in the production of sustainable, flexible, luminescent, and opaque membranes [26]. Patil et al. produced PVA nanocomposite films by incorporating CDs derived from waste tea residue. These CDs not only allow light transmission across the film but also efficiently block harmful UV rays achieving 100 % in both UVA and UVB regions and 60 % in the UVC region [27]. Campalani and his colleagues produced nanocomposite films based on gelatin using a solution casting method, incorporating carbon dots derived from fish biomass. They report that the inclusion of CDs significantly improved the tensile properties of the gelatin films, particularly increasing the tensile strength (TS) to 17 Mega Pascal with a 40 % elongation at break (EAB). The GL/5%CDs films effectively blocked over 70 % of UV light, resulting in a slight reduction in transparency and opacity [28].

In continuous effort to prepare active packaging films, the present study is carried out to prepare VNG-CDs incorporated CH/GL nanocomposite films. Vachellia nilotica gum is a cheaper and sustainable resource, and repared nano dots exhibit effective antibacterial and UV-blocking capabilities. The resulting film exhibits enhanced optical, physical and mechanical characteristics when compared to a hydrogel film made solely from chitosan and gelatin. These nanocomposite films underwent thorough analysis of their optical, thermal and biological properties, to assess their suitability for UV-protection, anti-oxidative, and antimicrobial food packaging. Furthermore, the efficacy of these films was assessed under diverse storage conditions, using bananas as a representative model of food, demonstrating the adaptability of VNG-CDs owing to their key attributes, such as ultraviolet resistance, low cytotoxicity, thermal stability, antioxidant and antibacterial properties.

Chitosan (degree of deacetylation ≥75 %), purified gelatin (fish skin), methanol, gallic acid, ascorbic acid, sodium hydroxide (NaOH), acetic acid, glycerol (d = 1.25 g/ml at 20 °C), Sodium nitrite (NaNO2), folin-ciocalteu reagent (FCR), 2,2-diphenyl-1-picrylhydrazyl (DPPH), sodium carbonate (Na2CO3), distilled water, and deionized water. All chemicals except distilled water were purchased from Sigma Aldrich. The VNG-CDs and CH/GL nanocomposite films have been authenticated by UV–visible spectrophotometer (Agilent Cary 60, 200–1000 nm). The FTIR spectra were obtained at B54Bruker ATR (IR spectrophotometer). Spectra were obtained in the range of 4000–650 cm−1 with 4 cm−1 resolution. Surface morphology and chemical composition was established by SEM and EDX (FEI Nova 450 Nano SEM) analysis. Scanning Electron Microscopy (SEM) of VNG-CDs was conducted at 10 kV with different magnifications. The phase identification and crystalline structure of VNG-CDs and CH/GL nanocomposite films were investigated using X-ray diffraction (XRD). The XRD spectrum of VNG-CDs and films were obtained from Bruker D8 Advance powder X-rays diffractometer (Bruker D2 phaser equipped with the unique LYNXEYE XE-T detector). The diffractograms were recorded at 2θ values between 10 and 80° at 30 kV and 10 mA using Cu Kα. [29]. Luminescence studies were performed by using Agilent Cary Eclipse spectrophotometer.

The VNG-CDs were synthesized by hydrothermal carbonization of vachellia nilotica gum. 2 g of powdered gum in 40 mL deionized water stirred for 24 h. The reaction mixture was transferred to 100 mL Teflon lined stainless steel autoclave and heated at 190 °C for 16 h. Afterwards CDs were allowed to cool naturally to room temperature and filtered through a 0.45 μm followed by 0.22 μm whatmann filter paper to remove large particle. The filtrate was centrifuged at room temperature at 12,000 rpm for 30 min to remove large particle. The supernatant was evaporated and dried at room temperature to obtain CDs as brown solid. Finally, the dried CDs stored at 0 °C for further characterization and applications.

CH/GL-CDs nanocomposite films were prepared using the solution casting method [30]. Chitosan (6G) dissolved in 300 mL of 2 % acetic acid solution and stirred for 12 h to get mixture I. Gelatin (6 g) dissolved in 300 mL of deionized water with continuous stirring yielded mixture II. Cheese cloth was used to filter the resulting solutions. These two mixtures I and II (50:50, v/v) were mixed together with no heat by constantly stirring at 500 rpm for 30 min. In 100 mL of the blended solution was subsequently added VNG-CDs at 1 %, 2 %, 3 %, 4 % and 5 % w/w(CH/GL)separately to get CH/GL-CDs1%, CH/GL-CDs2%, CH/GL-CDs3%, CH/GL-CDs4% and CH/GL-CDs5% nanocomposite films respectively. Furthermore, 25 % (w/w) glycerol was transferred to serve as a plasticizer to each solution, which was then agitated at 500 rpm for 15 min. To achieve a satisfactory blend, all mixtures were heated and stirred at 45 °C for 2 h and the film forming solution (FFS) was obtained. Then, 20 mL of FFS was casted in70 mm glass petri-dishes and allowed to dry for 2 days on ambient conditions. In the same way, a control film without CH/GL-CDs was also prepared. The films were carefully removed from the petri dishes and stored in airtight plastic bags for further characterization and applications. Design and preparation of films is depicted in Fig. 1 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Thickness, water solubility and water vapor permeability of CS/GL-CDs nanocomposite films
ce:section.ce:para:
Thickness of the film was measured by a micrometer. For each film, thickness was randomly measured in triplicate at three different positions. To calculate the water solubility (WS %) of films, a modified method of Campalani et al. was adopted [28]. Small film samples (2 × 2 cm^2) were dried at 60 °C until a constant weight was achieved (24 h). The samples were placed in beakers with 15 mL of distilled water for 24 h, filtered the solution, and dried the remaining film residues at 60 °C for 24 h. Water solubility was then calculated using equation as following
Water solubility
WS %
=
Wi − Wf
Wi
× 100
Where Wi is the initial weight of the film (as dry matter) and Wf refer to the undissolved dried film residue weight. All tests were carried out in duplicate [28].Water vapor permeability values were determined using a modified ASTM E96 (2017) technique. The film was sealed on a conical flask containing silica gel (0 % RH) with rubber band to hold the film in place. The conical flask was placed, at 20 °C in a desiccator with distilled water. The vapor pressure inside the cup and desiccator was 0 and 2337 Pa, respectively. The weight of the conical flasks was recorded over time. WVP value was calculated as the following;
WVP =
∆ M × X
A × T ×
P 2 − P 1
Where, ∆ M is weight gaining of the cup (g); X is the average film thickness (m); A is the area of wrapped film (m2); T
is the time at weight gaining (s); and
P 2 − P 1
is the difference in vapor pressure around the film (Pa). This whole procedure was repeated twice on each film [31].

The antioxidant activity of VNG-CDs and CS/GL-CDs nanocomposite films were evaluated by DPPH (2,2-diphenyl-1-picrylhydrazyl) free radicals scavenging method with some modifications [32]. The 0.1 mM DPPH solution (control) was prepared by dissolving 0.004 g of DPPH in 100 ml of methanol. For generation of free radicals, DPPH solution was placed in dark for 1 h. Sample solutions of different concentrations (100, 200, 300, 400 and 500 μg/mL) of VNG-CDs and CS/GL-CDs1–5 % films were prepared. The reaction mixture containing 100 μL of each sample with 2700 μL of DPPH solution was incubated in dark for half an hour. Absorbance of the mixture was measured at 517 nm and methanol was used as blank. Ascorbic acid was taken as standard [33].

The scavenging activity of DPPH free radicals were calculated by the following equation:
DPPH inhibition
%
=
Abs
of Control − Abs
of Sample
Abs
of Control
× 100

The measurement of total reducing capability of VNG-CDs and CH/GL-CDs film samples was determined through measurement of total phenolic content (TPC). Folin-ciocalteu's colorimetric technique was employed for TPC estimation. Samples comprising 0.01 g/mL concentration of VNG-CDs and CS/GL/CDs film making components were prepared in deionized water. Initially, 0.5 mL of samples was stirred with 2.5 mL of Folin Ciocalteu reagent (10 times diluted) and 2 mL of Na2CO3 (7.5 %) was added into it. The reaction mixture was incubated for 30 min at room temperature. The absorbance of reaction mixture was recorded at a wavelength of 760 nm using a spectrophotometer. The phenolic contents of the fractions were detected using a gallic acid standard curve, and the results were expressed in mg gallic acid equivalents per gram dry weight of fraction (mg GAE/g) [34].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Packaging application of CS/GL nanocomposite films on real sample (banana)
ce:section.ce:para: Uniform, blemish-free bananas were selected from the store. After inspection fresh, cleaned, and dried bananas were individually wrapped with nanocomposite films CH/GL-CDs1%, CH/GL-CDs2%, CH/GL-CDs3%, CH/GL-CDs4% and CH/GL-CDs5%. One banana sample remained unwrapped to serve as the control. All samples were stored at room temperature (25 °C) in a clean environment to observe the impact of the films on banana samples. During the one-month period, various characteristics of the bananas were monitored on a daily basis. Photographs of all the bananas were taken and were used for visual evaluation of any alteration in the condition of the bananas.

Thickness measurements, WS, VWP permeability, and transmittance data is the average of the triplicate analysis. Results recorded as mean ± standard error of mean (SEM). One way ANOVA and tukey's multiple comparison tests were used for results analyses. p values <0.05 were considered as significant.

The optical properties of green fluorescent VNG-CDs were studies by UV − Visible spectroscopy (Fig. 2a). The VNG-CDs solution was transparent and brown in day light and turned to green fluorescent solution upon excitation under UV light of 360 nm wavelength (Fig. 2b). In UV–Visible spectrum, two absorption bands at 225 nm and 280 nm were observed and attributed to π- π* and n-π* transition of the CC bonds and CO bond respectively.

The fluorescence emission spectra of the VNG-CDs were recorded at a concentration of 1 mg/ml upon excitation at different wavelengths from 340 to 450 nm as shown in the Fig. 3a. The maximum fluorescent intensity was observed under λex = 360 nm with the maximum emission at 423 nm. In the fluorescent emission spectrum at different wavelengths it is evident that emission spectrum is slightly dependent on excitation wavelengths. Here red to blue and then red shift was observed for the values fluctuating between 413 and 435 nm. Intensity of the emission spectra lied in the range between 350 and 550 nm. The maximum λex of VNG-CDs observed at 360 nm which gradually decreases as the wavelength increases. The PL intensities of VNG-CDs were measured at 340, 360, 380, 420 and 450 nm respectively with maximum absorption of UV rays at 360 nm (λmax 424 nm). The PL intensity depends upon the number of particles excited at specific wavelengths (S. [35]). Hence Fig. 3b affirms that intensity of the excitation is decreased as the concentration decreases. Since the solvent has a considerable influence on the photoluminescence of carbon dots, deionized water was utilized for their better luminescence.

The morphology and size of VNG-CDs was determined from scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images (Fig. 4a-c). The size of the particles was measured by statistical analysis of >100 particles using Image J software. Fig. 4d represents the diameter distribution of VNG-CDs and the guassian curve represents the average diameter of 7.2 nm. As the CDs are prepared from natural gum which is a soft polymeric material, therefore the diameter is slightly greater than other CDs from natural sources. Literature reveals that carbon dots prepared from polymeric materials are larger in size which results in light emission at greater wavelength (Fig. 5 ).

The FT-IR spectrum of VNG-CDs showed presence of various functional groups of the gum at the surface of CDs. Characteristic peaks at 1057 and 995 cm−1 observed due to CO stretching and CC bending vibrations (Fig. 6a). A stretching band at 2926 cm−1 showed the presence of COOH group. The absorption band at 1762 cm−1 and 1576 cm−1 due to the CO and CC stretching vibration. The absorption band at 3171 cm−1 shows the presence of OH and NH stretching vibrations. The band due to CH stretching vibrations appeared at 2926 cm−1.

The XRD graph showed that VNG-CDs had a broad and dispersed peak at 2θ value of 21.9° due to the graphitic carbon crystal gap that spreads from 14.7° to 32.8° (Fig. 6b). This showed that VNG-CDs have relatively low degree of crystalline structure. The TGA curve of VNG-CDs showed that they are thermally stable as lost only 3 % weight at 120 °C which, could be due to the loss of the moisture taken from surroundings (Fig. 6c). The VNG-CDs showed two distinct curves of the greatest breakdown in range of 120 to 330 °C and 330 to 800 °C. The loss of 43 % of weight at 322 °C is due to the degradation of surface functional groups of the VNG-CDs as carbonyl, carboxyl, amine and etc.

The disintegration of carbonaceous materials occurred between 350 and 800 °C temperature [36]. In cytotoxicity analysis, the hemolysis percentage measures the extent of harm inflicted on red blood cells by VNG-CDs (Fig. 6d). Higher hemolysis suggests more harm and potential cytotoxic effects. Triton-X serves as a standard reference Notably, VNG-CDs exhibit significantly lower hemolysis than Triton-X which is preferable and indicate that VNG-CDs are safer to red blood cells when compared with Triton-X.

The average thickness of the control film CH/GL was measured as 65.7 μm. When we added VNG-CDs to the film, the thickness of the film increased as the concentration of CDs increased. This increase in thickness can be attributed to the higher density of the solution, which is influenced by the interaction between carbon dots, chitosan, and gelatin in the polymer matrix of the film. Mei et al. also reported that the thickness of silk sericin (SS) and chitosan (CS) based film increased with the addition of carbon dots prepared from cocoon silk [37].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Water solubility and water vapor permeability
ce:section.ce:para: The control CH/GL film exhibited water solubility of 44.9 ± 0.66 % which decreased by incorporation of minor quantity of carbon dots (1 to 2 % CDs). Although CNG-CDs are water-soluble but the OH groups on their exterior surface generated coupling interaction with each other and result in the development of new chains with low-molecular components in the films which limit their solubility. The components of the films, or the interaction between their hydrophilicity and hydrophobicity, and subsequently their structure, are directly related to the water solubility of the films [38]. When more VNG-CDs are added, the WS increased from 66.5 ± 0.36 % to 70.9 ± 0.94 % because of the polar character of C-dots which raised the solubility of films in water. In the creation CDs-based gelatin films, Campalani and Causin (2022) achieved outcomes that were comparable to the results of this study, with WS values ranging from 70.1 to 69.9 % [28]. WVP is an essential feature of packaging. The control film (CGCF) had WVP of 5.9 ± 0.06 × 10−11 g− 1 s−1 Pa−1 which decreased from 5.6 to 3.9 × 10−11 g− 1 s−1 Pa−1 with addition of VNG-CDs from 1 to 2 % in CH/GL solution. The VNG-CDs decreased the rate at which water molecules diffuse through the films by reducing the free volume of the CS/GL medium and lowering WVP values. The permeability of films increased from 6.4 to 7.4 × 10−11 g− 1 s−1 Pa−1 and remained constant as the concentration of VNG-CDs further enhanced (3 % to 5 %). These films seemed to have a very high WVP, while still lower than the values recorded for gelatin films prepared from fish skin (11.1 × 10−11 g− 1 s−1 Pa−1) [39] (Table 1 ).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties (UV visible and transmittance) of CH/GL-CDs nanocomposite films
ce:section.ce:para:
UV–Visible absorption spectra of CH/GL-CDs nanocomposite films showed that incorporation of CDs enhanced the absorption capacity of films maximum in CS/GL-CDs5% ( Fig. 7
a ). However the control CS/GL film exhibited limited absorbance in the UV area from 200 to 400 nm. The UV–visible analysis was also performed in transmittance (%) format to determine the UV blocking property of prepared CS/GL-CDs nanocomposite films with different concentrations of CDs ( Fig. 7
b).The transmittance of light for CS/GL-CDs films in ultraviolet (UV-A, UV-B and UV-C) and visible regions gradually decreased after the incorporation of various VNG-CDs concentrations (1–5 %). In the visible region (540 nm) the optical transmittance of control film (CH/GL) was found to be maximum 91.56 % which decreased by addition of VNG-CDs. The nanocomposite films CS/GL-CDs1%, CS/GL-CDs2%, CS/GL-CDs3%, CS/GL-CDs4%, CS/GL-CDs5% showed optical transmittance as 90.68 %, 83.08 %, 80.19 %, 80.02 % and 72.94 % respectively.

The control CS/GL film transmitted light 81.19 % in UVA (340 nm), 76.60 % in UVB (300 nm) and 68.55 % in UVC (255 nm) region respectively. It means that pure CS/GL film also has the ability to block 20–30 % of UV light. The CS/GL-CDs1–5 % nanocomposite films transmitted light by 80.61 %, 69.04 %, 62.66 % 57.87 % and 46.88 % of UVA (340 nm) region, 74.09 %, 61.96 %, 54.46 %, 48.35 % and 35.69 % of UVB (300 nm) region and 64.38 %, 51.04 %, 42.43 %, 36.10 % and 24.07 % of UVC (255 nm) region respectively (Table 2 ).

It was examined that CS/GL-CDs5% film demonstrated the greatest UV protection capacity and blocked 30–60 % light in UVA (318–400 nm), 60–70 % in UVB (280–318 nm) and almost 90 % light in UVC (200–280 nm) region. All this data demonstrated that adding VNG-CDs to CS/GL nanocomposite films can be employed as an effective ultraviolet light absorber in the fabrication of transparent UV-blocking films. As a result, to avoid photo-oxidation, food packaging might employ composite films [15,37,40].

The FTIR spectral analysis has been employed to investigate the skeletal and physicochemical modifications that occurred after the insertion of CDs into the CS/GL films (Fig. 8 ). The FTIR spectrum of the CS/GL-CDs films revealed NH (amine I & II) and OH stretching between 3431 and 3259 cm−1 of chitosan and gelatin coupled via H-bonding. The peak due to the CH stretching is observed at 2927 cm−1. The CO stretching vibration at 1636 cm−1 showed the presence of amide group. This peak also confirms the existence of beta-sheets in secondary framework of gelatin within CS/GL-CDs film [12]. The adjacent peaks from 1541 to 1239 cm−1 can be caused by a combination of NH bending (amide II) –OH bending and CN stretching vibrations. The CO stretching and C-O-C anti-symmetric stretching appears at 1075 and 991.5 cm−1 present in the polysaccharide chains of CS/GL matrix. The peak intensity of CS/GL-CDs get lowered which reflecting the development of chitosan, gelatin and glycerol bonding interactions. If they do not interact, the absorption peaks must be similar to their original spectra[6].

The FTIR spectrum of CS/GL-CDs 1 % has broad and more intense peaks at 3263, 1653, 1559, 1075 and 995 cm−1 as compared to control CH/GL film. In CS/GL-CDs1% spectrum the absorption peak at 3263 cm−1is due to OH and NH stretching. The Peak at 2926 cm−1 shows the CH stretching. The carbonyl of amide groups as well as β-pleated sheets of gelatin similar to CH/GL displayed a peak at 1653 cm−1. The CN and OH stretching along with NH bending appears between 1457 and 1205 cm−1. The CO stretch and C-O-C anti-symmetric stretch appears from 1075 to 926.2 cm−1. As the concentration of carbon dots increased then CS/GL-CDs 3 %, CS/GL-CDs 4 % and CS/GL-CDs 5 % spectrum showed the shifting of OH and NH stretching from 3263 to 3274 cm−1, C-O-C stretch from 995 to 1017 cm−1 and CO stretching appears at same position (1636 cm−1) with high intensities. The change in intensity and shifting of peaks are due to the incorporation of VNG-CDs which illustrate the H-bonding among different groups (CO, -COOH, -NH,-OH) on surface of carbon dots, gelatin, chitosan and glycerol.

The effect of incorporation of carbon quantum dots and their interaction with the CS/GL films were examined through the XRD analysis (Fig. 9 ) [41]. The CH/GL film shows a dominant and broad peak at 2θ = 22.6° and a minor peak near 2θ = 11.5°. Clear chitosan films, on the other hand, contain strong peaks that reveal their crystalline composition. However, the wider peaks in CS/GL diffraction spectrum demonstrate that GL degraded the crystalline nature of CS in the manufacturing process and ensures all components are properly blended [42]. The crystal structure of the CS/GL films was not substantially altered with the addition of carbon dots. The CS/GL-CDs 1 % to 5 % show broad peak at 20.3°, 22.1°, 22.5°, 22.3° and 23.1° respectively and minor peak at same position (11.5°) for all samples with decreased intensity than CH/GL. As, the concentration of carbon dots increased in CS/GL-CDs 1 % to 5 %, it appears that the peaks grew a little wider. So, the inclusion of nanoparticles (VNG-CDs) can be detected by the widening of the XRD peaks of the films.

Thermo-gravimetric analysis has been utilized for analyzing the thermal degradation behavior and stability of the carbon dots and CS/GL-CDs nanocomposite films [2] (Fig. 10 ). The CS/GL-CDs 5 % film exhibits three phases of weight loss in thermo-gravimetric analysis. The initially observed phase of losing weight of approximately 5–7 % started to occur in a temperature range of 75 to 85 °C. It is possible because of the excess moisture evaporation in the film. Another weight drop (13–15 %) has been noticed between 100 and 190 °C temperature, which might be a result of the deterioration of glycerol, interlinked water molecules and small peptides. The final thermal deterioration of CS/GL framework occurred at about 380 °C temperature. Because of the greater interactions among chitosan, gelatin and VNG-CDs which limit its deterioration, the thermal stability of CS/GL-CDs 5 % film enhanced after the incorporation of VNG-CDs as compared to reported pure CS/GL film [13,43,44].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antibacterial activities of VNG-CDs and nanocomposite films
ce:section.ce:para: The carbon dots can be used to combat bacteria due to their highly positive surfaces and extremely small size. The antimicrobial activities of VNG-CDs and CG/GL nanocomposite films against Staphylococcus aureus, Escherichia coli and Klebsiella pneumonia have been measured in terms of their IC50 values. A lower IC50 value demonstrates greater antibacterial activity. The bacterial efficacy of VNG-CDs is almost similar to or even greater than the traditional antibacterial agents such as ciprofloxacin especially against S. aureus. Bacteriostatic effect of VNG-CDs is more spectral and obvious on drug resistant bacterial strains and will not cause bacterial resistance. The presence of amino groups on the surface, presence of ultra-high positive charge and small particle size of VNG-CDs contributed to the quick binding of CDs on bacteria, destroying the surface charge and then entering the bacteria to kill them.

Accordingly, the antibacterial efficacy of VNG-CDs incorporated films increased with increasing concentration of nano dots. These results indicate the efficacy of nano dot films as bacterial barrier for packed food (Fig. 11 ).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antioxidant and reducing activities of VNG-CDs and nanocomposite films
ce:section.ce:para: The DPPH (2,2-diphenyl-1-picrylhydrazyl) assay is a widely used method to measure the antioxidant activity of compounds [34] (Fig. 12a). The control CS/GL film demonstrated a relatively low radical scavenging activity of 4.25 % at a concentration 500 μg/mL. However, upon the incorporation of VNG-CDs into the CH/GL matrix, the antioxidant properties were notably improved. Specifically, CS/GL-CDs 5 % exhibited the highest radical scavenging activity of 17.51 % at 500 μg/mL. Additionally, pure VNG-CDs solution displayed a maximum DPPH% inhibition value of 37.13 % at 500 μg/ml concentration, highlighting the significant antioxidant potential of VNG-CDs, which enhanced the overall antioxidant activity of the chitosan/gelatin films. In comparison to the control CS/GL film, the VNG-CDs-based films displayed that antioxidant activity of the films is due to the activity of added VNG-CDs. Research indicates that the amount of antioxidants applied to packaging sheets directly correlates with their antioxidant activity [45]. The findings align with a prior study that observed a rise in %DPPH inhibition as the concentration of nanoparticles increased [29].

Phenolic compounds have the ability to reduce certain molecules by donating electrons. The Folin-Ciocalteu method was employed to assess the reducing capacity in terms of total phenolic content, in VNG-CDs and CS/GL-CDs films featuring varying concentrations of VNG-CDs (Fig. 12b). The VNG-CDs has maximum value of TPC (44.74 ± 0.19 mg GAE/g). The TPC of the control film was 17.52 ± 0.19 mg GAE/g, whereas it increased to 41.3 ± 0 mg GAE/g by incorporating 5 % VNG-CDs in CS/GL film. The results indicated that CH/GL film has lowest reducing capability which is significantly increased by increasing the concentration of carbon dots in CG/GL nanocomposite films and suggest that these antioxidant materials can delay the oxidation process of packed, prone to oxidation foods by scavenging free radicals. This result coincides with Shaaban and Mahmoud [46], who illustrated that increasing EO concentration substantially enhanced the overall phenolic content in the S-CH films [46].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of nanocomposite films to monitor banana freshness
ce:section.ce:para: We conducted an investigation into the potential impact of VNG-CDs-incorporated CH/GLfilms on the extended preservation of bananas as presented in Fig. 13 . Our research involved subjecting bananas to store at 23 °C and analyzing the results daily. The bananas were individually wrapped in various films, including CH/GL, CS/GL-CDs1%, CS/GL-CDs2%, CS/GL-CDs3%, CS/GL-CDs4%, and CS/GL-CDs5%, while one group remained unwrapped as the control. To assess the level of oxidation, we examined the occurrence of black regions or black spots on the banana skins. It was observed that bananas enclosed in VNG-CDs-based films (CS/GL-CDs1–5 %) remained fresh, whereas the control group developed black spots and deteriorated within 7 days of storage. Bananas wrapped with the CH/GL film exhibited fewer black spots than the control group. After 15 days of storage, the unwrapped bananas had almost entirely deteriorated, emitting an unpleasant odor due to the breakdown of sugars and compounds as they became overripe. Ethylene gas production during ripening contributed to their rapid decay. Interestingly, bananas enclosed in VNG-CDs-based films remained in better condition, while those wrapped in CH/GL control film without VNG-CDs showed similar damage.

Over the course of a month, the bananas experienced size reduction and weight loss due to water loss, particularly evident when left unwrapped, where fungal growth occurred on the skin. Mold growth was also observed on bananas covered with the CS/GL control film. Notably, VNG-CDs-based films, particularly CS/GL-CDs1% and CS/GL-CDs2%, demonstrated an inhibitory effect on mold growth. However, bananas wrapped in CS/GL-CDs3–5 % exhibited minimal fungus strains, possibly attributed to the increasing carbon dot concentration, which heightened water solubility and permeability. This experiment's results featured the ability of VNG-CDs-based films to extend the shelf life of bananas by up to 15 days while effectively inhibiting fungal growth. It became evident that the prepared carbon dots possessed antifungal properties, and the CH/GL-CDs films displayed excellent attributes such as water vapor resistance, antimicrobial, antioxidant and UV-blocking capabilities.

In summary, the VNG-CDs were prepared from Vachellia nilotica gum, an environment friendly and non-toxic source. The CDs had quasi spherical shape with average diameter of 7.2 nm. The synthesized VNG-CDs were incorporated into chitosan/gelatin biopolymer matrix and developed into nanocomposite films. FTIR, SEM and XRD investigations all supported the proper dispersion of VNG-CDs and their appropriate interactions with chitosan and gelatin. The addition of VNG-CDs significantly improved the physicochemical properties, thermal stability, and structural integrity of the nanocomposite films as compared to CH/GL control films. The excellent antibacterial activity of the nanocomposite films as well as remarkable antioxidant and reducing capabilities qualify them a suitable candidate as active packaging with microbial control. Measurement of the UV blocking property of the hybrid films also suggest them suitable UV blockers (UVC:90 %, UVB:70 %, UVA:60 %). As a test case, Bananas were packed in the nanocomposite films demonstrated considerable delay in size reduction, weight loss, microbial growth and ripening and authenticated active packaging potential of films. This study suggested that VNG-CDs incorporated biodegradable CS/GL films could serve as multipurpose packaging material that reduce food waste while enhancing both sustainability and food preservation.

Shehla Parveen: Conceptualization, Investigation, Project administration, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Sadia Nazeer: Methodology. Ghayoor Abbas Chotana: Formal analysis. Aqsa Kanwal: Data curation. Benish Batool: Methodology. Naeema Bukhari: Software. Asma Yaqoob: Methodology. Faiza Talib: Data curation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: Authors declare no competing financial interests that could influence the work reported in this literature.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Kumar, S., Mudai, A., Roy, B., Basumatary, I. B., Mukherjee, A., & Dutta, J. (2020). Biodegradable hybrid nanocomposite of chitosan/gelatin and green synthesized zinc oxide nanoparticles for food packaging. Foods, 9(9), 1143.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Edible film with antioxidant capacity based on salmon gelatin and boldine
LWT

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: López, D., Márquez, A., Gutiérrez-Cutiño, M., Venegas-Yazigi, D., Bustos, R., & Matiacevich, S. (2017). Edible film with antioxidant capacity based on salmon gelatin and boldine. Lwt, 77, 160-169.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: In-vitro antibacterial and antioxidant properties of starch/chitosan edible composite film incorporated with thyme essential oil
J. Arab Soc. Med. Res.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Shaaban, H. A., & Mahmoud, K. F. (2014). In-vitro antibacterial and antioxidant properties of starch/chitosan edible composite film incorporated with thyme essential oil. Journal of The Arab Society for Medical Research, 9(2), 54-61.
